data.title: West Pharma upgraded at Deutsche Bank despite earnings led selloff
data.description:
Deutsche Bank upgraded West Pharmaceutical Services (NYSE:WST) to Buy from Hold on Friday, even after the contract drug manufacturer witnessed a ~38% drop in the previous session following a worse-than-expected outlook issued with its Q4 2024 financials.

DB analysts argued that post-selloff, West Pharma (NYSE:WST) is trading at an attractive entry point given its compelling moat in the injectable-drug containment market, where recurring revenue tends to be sticky.

“The stock is now trading close to pre-pandemic levels and at the lower end of its historical trading range over the last 5–10 years,” Deutsche Bank wrote, projecting a 20% internal rate of return (IRR) for the stock over two years under a base case scenario.

Despite forecasting several outcomes where returns range from high single-digit to mid-30% returns over the period, the analysts slashed their price target on West Pharma (NYSE:WST) to $250 from $275 per share.

The firm attributed the revision to a new target multiple that reflects the company’s extended recovery period and downward revision cycle, with a 15% discount to the stock’s five-year average.

data.title: CAE jumps as activist investor, others to board
data.description:
CAE Inc. (NYSE:CAE) rose 14% as the flight simulator firm added an activist investor and two other directors to its board.

CAE is adding Peter Lee to its board under an agreement with activist investor Browning West, according to a statement on Thursday. The company is also adding Louis Têtu to its board. Têtu was nominated by Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec, one of the company's largest holders. CAE is also adding Katherine A. Lehman to its board.

CAE also said it appointed former Air Canada CEO Calin Rovinescu as chair of the board.

In December, activist Browning West disclosed a stake in CAE (NYSE:CAE) and said it wanted to help the flight simulator find a new chief executive officer.

CAE announced in November that president and chief executive officer, Marc Parent, will be leaving the company at the annual general meeting in August.

CAE also reported Q3 results on Thursday.

data.title: CRISPR upped to outperform by Evercore on upcoming data
data.description:
Evercore ISI has upgraded CRISPR Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CRSP) to outperform from in line, citing upcoming catalysts.

The investment firm said it was now including the CTX320 and CTX310 in vivo programs in its price target, with probabilities of success of 10% and 5%, respectively. Data for CTX320 is expected in Q2, with data for CTX310 also expected this year.

Other catalysts include additional data and a regulatory update for CTX112 in B-cell malignancies, expected mid-year; data for CTX112 in SLE, also expected mid-year; a Phase 1 update for CTX131 in solid tumors and hematological malignancies, expected in Q4; and a first look at Phase 1 data for CTX221 in type 1 diabetes, expected in the second half of the year.

Evercore also noted that the company has guided for "strong growth" in cell collections for its treatment Casgevy in 2025. The product is co-marketed with Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX).

The firm raised its price target for the stock to $99 from $60.

data.title: Tomra Systems targets mid-single-digit growth in recycling and 5% in collection for 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tomra Systems ASA (OTCPK:TMRAY) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Tove Andersen highlighted record revenue and EBITDA for Q4 2024, driven by strong performance in all divisions. Collection revenues grew 2%, Recycling achieved a 37% increase, and Food delivered 13% growth. Gross margin improved to 46%, up from 44% in Q4 2023.
  • Andersen emphasized the €30 million cost savings achieved in the Food division through restructuring, which has now been finalized. Cash flow for the year reached €235 million.
  • Andersen announced a dividend proposal of NOK 2.15 per share, a 10% increase, adhering to the policy of a 40%-60% payout ratio.
  • CFO Eva Sagemo noted that operating expenses remained stable compared to the previous year, and Q4 EBITDA reached €78 million.

Outlook

  • Management expects Collection revenues in existing markets to grow by 5% in 2025, with significant contributions from Austria, Romania, and Poland. The Polish rollout is expected to span two years, with initial activity ramping up in 2025.
  • Recycling is projected to achieve mid-single-digit growth in 2025, supported by stable waste sorting activity, increased service sales, and growth in metals and mining. No recovery in the plastics segment is anticipated for the year.
  • Food is expected to see low single-digit growth in 2025. Profitability will remain a focus, with gross margins in the mid-40s and EBITDA margins aligned with Q4 2024 levels.
  • The Horizon portfolio, including the new Feedstock plant in Norway, is anticipated to deliver limited revenues initially but aims for positive EBITDA by the end of 2025.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue reached €398 million, a 12% increase compared to the same period last year. EBITDA rose by 46% to €78 million, driven by cost savings in Food and high volumes in Recycling.
  • Collection achieved revenues of €203 million, with growth driven by existing markets like the Americas and Australia, as well as new markets like Austria and Romania.
  • Recycling reported revenues of €103 million, supported by a strong order backlog of €107 million.
  • Food's revenues reached €91 million, reflecting a 13% increase, with notable aftermarket sales growth.

Q&A

  • Adela Dashian, Jefferies: Asked about the impact of delays in Poland's implementation and TOMRA's market share expectations there. CFO Eva Sagemo confirmed a likely back-end-loaded revenue contribution in 2025 but refrained from providing specific market share figures.
  • Elliott Jones, Danske Bank: Inquired about contributions from new markets like Austria and Romania. Sagemo confirmed continued rollout in these regions in 2025 but noted Hungary's activity would be less significant.
  • Victoria Adesina, Barclays: Asked about recovery expectations in Food and Recycling. CEO Andersen stated that Food shows signs of recovery in some categories, while no recovery is expected in plastics recycling.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, with concerns about Poland's timeline and competitive dynamics. They pressed for clarity on market share expectations and revenue timing.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing achievements in cost savings and market expansion. However, their responses in the Q&A were more measured, particularly when discussing Poland and market sentiment in Recycling.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management displayed consistent confidence, though analysts appeared more focused on potential risks in new market rollouts and order intake.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 revenue growth of 12% was stronger than the 6% growth in Q3. EBITDA margins also improved from 13% in Q3 to 46% in Q4, driven by cost savings and higher volumes.
  • Collection growth slowed from 14% in Q3 to 2% in Q4, while Recycling rebounded with a 37% increase compared to an 18% decline in Q3.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from cost-saving measures in Food to market expansion and order backlog conversion in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Analysts expressed concerns about delays in Poland's rollout and its impact on 2025 revenue. Management highlighted the variability in revenue depending on sales models.
  • Softer market sentiment in plastics recycling remains a challenge, with no recovery expected in 2025.
  • Competition in new markets like Poland and uncertainties around tender processes in Portugal and Spain were noted.

Final Takeaway

Tomra Systems delivered record revenue and EBITDA in Q4 2024, supported by strong performance across its divisions and effective cost-saving measures in Food. While the company anticipates mid-single-digit growth in Recycling and 5% growth in Collection for 2025, challenges such as market sentiment in plastics recycling and competitive dynamics in new markets like Poland remain critical areas to watch. Management's confidence in its strategic positioning suggests a focus on long-term opportunities despite short-term uncertainties.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Twilio tumbles even as Wall Street defends amid slowing growth
data.description:
Twilio's (NYSE:TWLO) fourth-quarter results and guidance may have disappointed investors, but Wall Street firms defended the communications software firm, as its financial future may be better initially perceived.

Shares were down around 16% in early trading on Friday.

“Guidance for Q1 imagines some retrenchment back below double digits to 8.4% [year-over-year], with a difficult leap-year comp, plus two fewer days to generate revenue in Q125 vs. Q424,” Bernstein analyst Peter Weed wrote in a note to clients. “We anticipate this is conservative, and they will likely get just over 10% growth, as the continued improvement in the economy drives commercial engagement for their customers, and thus increased consumption of the Communications service (while the Segment business sees some stabilization).”

Weed kept his Market Perform rating on Twilio, but raised his price target to $119 from $82 following the results.

Mizuho Securities analyst Siti Panigrahi had a similar train of thought, as the company ended 2024 with a “strong finish” and the expectation is the same for 2025.

“Following the operating model turnaround, strong new margin targets, and recent execution on top line, TWLO is delivering largely as we expected in our Jan 8 [upgrade],” Panigrahi explained. “We believe the top-line guidance remains conservative and the 2025/2027 margin targets do not embed upside from revenue outperformance (vs. 7-8% 'target'), which continues to create an attractive setup for TWLO shares.” Panigrahi reiterated his Outperform rating on Twilio and upped his price target to $165 from $150.

Needham analyst Ryan Koontz also said the company's core business is “humming,” but went a step further and said it is being bolstered by its “nascent” artificial intelligence exposure.

“With its core business now humming, TWLO aims to leverage its new [communications plus data plus AI] strategy to take a meaningful slice of the AI value chain for [customer service],” Koontz wrote. “We modestly adjust [up] our F25/F26 estimates and maintain our Buy rating and $165 PT.”

data.title: Wall Street hovers just under record intraday high, bonds surge after weak retail sales
data.description:
The major U.S. averages hugged the flat line on Friday, but Wall Street was on track to snap a two-week losing streak amid tariffs and inflation dominating headlines. Meanwhile, Treasury yields fell as traders snapped up bonds after a weak retail sales reading.

Over an hour into regular trading, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.05%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.09%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.15%.

Tariffs took center stage this week, with Trump on Monday announcing 25% levies on all steel and aluminum imports into the U.S. On Thursday, Trump signed a memorandum that tasked his administration to investigate current trade relations and propose reciprocal tariffs on a country-specific basis.

Still, the fact that Trump did not immediately put in effect the tit-for-tat tariffs and was scant on details, helped market sentiment and drove Wall Street more than 1% higher in Thursday's session. A favorable producer inflation report for January also helped.

On the surface, the data showed that the M/M increase in the headline producer price index (PPI) was higher than expectations, while the core PPI came in-line. However, digging deeper, the components in the PPI that directly feed into the core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index came in soft for January. That bodes well for the Federal Reserve's favorite inflation gauge.

The PPI update helped overcome the shock provided by hotter-than-expected consumer price index (CPI) readings on Wednesday.

"It was a love-in for markets yesterday with bonds and equities rallying following better-than-expected inflation data and news that details on reciprocal tariffs would not come before April. This led to a revival of hopes that the Fed would still cut rates this year, particularly after the more hawkish fears post the upside surprise in the CPI report the previous day," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

Treasury yields slipped on Friday after January retail sales came in significantly worse than expected. The 10-year yield (US10Y) dipped 7 basis points to 4.47%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 6 basis points to 4.26%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

After the closing bell, 13F filings will be in focus - regulatory disclosures by major funds of their quarterly equity ownership changes.

data.title: MTY Food Group projects digital sales growth amid restructuring and ERP launch in 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MTY Food Group Inc. (OTCPK:MTYFF) Q4 2024

Management View

  • Eric Lefebvre, CEO, highlighted a milestone achievement of net positive location growth for the first time in 10 years, with a net addition of 13 restaurants in Q4. This was achieved through 92 openings and 79 closures. He mentioned, “A strong pipeline of new locations and a good control of closures remained two of our top priorities for the future.”

  • Lefebvre emphasized the company’s dual growth strategy of leveraging strategic acquisitions and organic growth, despite not finding suitable acquisition targets in 2024. He also underlined the importance of ongoing restructuring and cost control measures to position MTY for long-term growth.

  • Renee St-Onge, CFO, reported normalized adjusted EBITDA of $59.4 million, a slight decrease of 1.6% year-over-year, while franchising segment margins increased to 51%, up from 47%. The corporate restaurant segment faced challenges, with EBITDA declining by 39% due to underperforming concepts like Barrio Queen and Granite City.

  • The company incurred a $64.6 million non-cash impairment charge, primarily on recently acquired corporate locations and intangible assets, along with a $26.3 million charge due to foreign exchange rate fluctuations.

  • Digital sales grew 9% year-over-year to $1.1 billion, now accounting for 20% of total sales. Lefebvre noted, “There’s still a lot of work to do to achieve our objectives, and we continue to take steps to make the customer experience as seamless and engaging as possible.”

Outlook

  • Management anticipates leveraging cost synergies and restructuring efforts to stabilize operations in 2025. Lefebvre remarked, “We anticipate more stability in 2025 as we hope to harvest the fruits of the cost control measures and synergies realized in 2024.”

  • The company is preparing for the rollout of a new ERP system later in 2025, expected to improve operational efficiency and support future growth.

  • Lefebvre reiterated that while network expansion progress is encouraging, future quarters may still see variability in net store growth.

Financial Results

  • MTY reported a normalized adjusted EBITDA of $59.4 million for Q4, compared to $60.4 million in the prior-year period. Franchising segments drove a 7.9% year-over-year increase in normalized adjusted EBITDA, offset by challenges in corporate restaurant margins.

  • System sales remained flat at $5.6 billion for fiscal 2024, though sequential improvement was noted, with Q4 sales increasing by 2%.

  • The company reported a net loss attributable to owners of $55.3 million, or $3.34 per diluted share, primarily due to impairment charges and foreign exchange losses.

  • Free cash flows net of rent payments totaled $5.75 per share for the year, highlighting strong cash flow generation.

Q&A

  • Anshul Agarwala, National Bank, asked about franchisee sentiment and potential opportunities for store growth. Lefebvre responded, “A large portion of our new store openings comes from existing franchisees. It’s about two-thirds…to us, that’s the validation we need to show that our brands are relevant.”

  • Agarwala also inquired about current quarter trends amid weather challenges and tax holidays. Lefebvre said, “In Q1, our Canadian restaurants have performed exceptionally well. In the U.S., it’s been a little bit more challenging due to weather.”

  • Derek Lessard, TD Cowen, sought clarity on 2025 outlook and network expansion. Lefebvre stated, “We’re off to a good start of 2025…we have the foundations to have other quarters of positive store growth, but negative growth might still happen.”

  • Lessard questioned the impairment charge on Papa Murphy’s. Lefebvre explained, “The performance was a little bit less in 2024…lowered our existing store base, and that caused a situation where realizable value becomes less.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly skeptical, with questions focusing on challenges in corporate restaurant performance, impairment charges, and macroeconomic conditions.

  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing strong cash flow generation, restructuring progress, and future growth initiatives. Lefebvre’s statement, “We feel really confident about our ability to generate organic growth,” underscores this sentiment.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management appeared more optimistic about restructuring benefits and digital sales growth, while analysts remained cautious about external challenges and underperforming brands.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, though Q4 highlighted optimism about digital sales and restructuring benefits in 2025.

  • Analysts’ focus shifted from general macroeconomic concerns in Q3 to specific issues such as impairment charges, corporate restaurant challenges, and weather impacts in Q4.

  • Key metrics showed a slight decline in normalized adjusted EBITDA and system sales growth compared to Q3, while digital sales growth and franchising margins improved.

  • Management’s tone was more optimistic in Q4, driven by digital sales performance and restructuring progress.

Risks and Concerns

  • Challenges included underperforming corporate restaurant concepts, impairment charges, and impacts from weather conditions in key markets.

  • Management highlighted mitigation efforts, including initiatives to improve corporate restaurant profitability and ongoing restructuring to optimize operations.

  • Analysts raised concerns about external factors such as tariffs, unfavorable weather, and macroeconomic uncertainty affecting growth.

Final Takeaway

MTY Food Group’s Q4 earnings call highlighted progress in digital sales, restructuring, and cost control, helping to offset challenges in corporate restaurant performance and macroeconomic headwinds. Management’s focus on leveraging digital growth, stabilizing operations, and executing its ERP rollout positions the company for improved performance in 2025, while ongoing acquisition efforts remain a key priority.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Glencore held talks over sale of multibillion-dollar Congo copper mines - FT
data.description:
Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY) said Friday it rejected an unsolicited approach from an unnamed buyer for its operations in the Democratic Republic of Congo at the end of last year.

Financial Times had reported that Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY) was considering selling a part or all of its Congo assets, which have been valued by RBC at $6.8B, and had held preliminary discussions about its multibillion-dollar copper and cobalt mines in the region.

The miner rejected an unsolicited bid for the mines from a potential buyer in the Middle East because the offer was too low, but it would consider a sale for the right price, according to FT.

Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY) owns the Mutanda copper-cobalt mine and a 75% stake in Kamoto Copper; the Congolese mines produced 225K metric tons of copper and 35K tons of cobalt last year, making the group the world’s second-biggest cobalt producer.

The global rush for copper has triggered a wave of M&A activity among the major miners, but the Congo mines have been far less profitable than Glencore’s (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY) other copper assets, generating just $195M of earnings in 2023 on revenues of $2.4B, due to operating setbacks and low cobalt prices.

Seperately, Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY) also recently held informal talks with potential buyers over the future of its assets in Kazakhstan; last year, the company abandoned a sale process last year for a large zinc, lead and gold producer in which it holds a 70% stake, which RBS has valued at $5.1B.
